flashcards.front: Define a deque and state its full form.
flashcards.back: Deque stands for Double-Ended Queue. It is a linear data structure that allows insertion and deletion of elements at both ends — the front and the rear.

An input-restricted deque allows insertion only at one end (usually rear) but allows deletion from both ends. Mathematically: insertFront() is prohibited, but insertRear(), deleteFront(), deleteRear() are allowed.

An output-restricted deque allows insertion at both ends but allows deletion only from one end (usually front). Mathematically: deleteFront() is prohibited, but insertFront(), insertRear(), deleteRear() are allowed.

flashcards.front: Prove that insertFront operation in a circular array deque runs in O(1) time.
flashcards.back: In circular array implementation, we maintain two pointers (front and rear) and use modulo arithmetic for wrap-around. InsertFront simply decrements front (with wrap-around) and stores the element at the new front position. This involves a single decrement, one modulo operation, and one array write — all constant-time operations. Therefore, T(n) = Θ(1).

flashcards.front: What is the space complexity of a deque implemented using a circular array of size n?
flashcards.back: The space complexity is O(n) where n is the maximum capacity. Each of the n positions can hold one element, and we maintain O(1) additional pointers (front, rear).

flashcards.front: State the conditions for overflow and underflow in a deque.
flashcards.back: Overflow (Full): When (rear + 1) mod n == front (for array-based implementation). Underflow (Empty): When front == -1 (or when front == rear after deletion in some implementations).

flashcards.front: How does a linked-list based deque differ from array-based in time complexity?
flashcards.back: In linked-list implementation: insertFront, insertRear, deleteFront, deleteRear all take O(1) time because we maintain pointers to both ends. Array-based also achieves O(1) but may require modulo operations for circular handling.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between a deque and a priority queue?
flashcards.back: In a deque, elements are inserted at ends and follow FIFO/LIFO order. In a priority queue, elements are inserted in arbitrary order but removed based on priority (highest/lowest priority first), not insertion order.

flashcards.front: What is the advantage of circular array implementation over linear array for deques?
flashcards.back: Circular implementation eliminates the 'creeping' problem where elements keep shifting. It allows utilization of freed spaces at the front, effectively doubling the capacity utilization. The front and rear pointers wrap around using modulo n arithmetic.

flashcards.front: List four real-world applications of deques.
flashcards.back: 1) Undo-Redo operations in text editors (using two deques). 2) Palindrome checking. 3) Task scheduling in operating systems. 4) Implementing stacks and queues simultaneously. 5) Railway coach rearrangement problem. 6) Maximum element sliding window problems.

flashcards.front: Define the ADT specification for deleteRear operation in a deque.
flashcards.back: deleteRear(): If deque is empty, throw underflow error. Otherwise, retrieve element at rear position, decrement rear (with wrap-around for circular), update rear. If after deletion front > rear, reset both to -1 (empty state). Return the deleted element. Time: O(1).

flashcards.front: What is the amortized complexity of dynamic array resizing in a deque?
flashcards.back: Using geometric resizing (doubling capacity), the amortized cost of n insertions is O(1) per operation. Proof: Each element is moved at most once during doubling, and the total cost of n operations is O(n), giving amortized O(1) per operation.
